title: Power BI Dashboard of US Accidents: 2016 - 2020
description: Road traffic crashes are a leading cause of death in the United States for people aged 1–54. The most recent data available from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration shows an average of 6,296,000 police-reported traffic crashes in the United States each year. Nationwide, an average of 3 million people is injured each year in car accidents, and over 90 people die in car accidents every day.  In the COVID-19 era, fatal accident percentages are up this year despite the smaller number of vehicles on the roads, NHTSA's latest data shows. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) offers up another reason we need to get rid of COVID-19: “reckless driving is up and causing more deaths.” As the number of car accidents occurring in the United States continues to increase, it can be expected that the number of people who die in car crashes will also continue to increase. This project aims to analyze the US accident data from 50 states to inform the US government agencies and the public on trends and possible causes of traffic accidents and what could be done to reduce them. The analysis includes; Accidents per Day, Percentage High Severity YOY Accidents, YOY Severity, best time to travel by month, day and hour, and factors like weather conditions responsible for accidents nationwide.
stepByStepContent:
Power BI Dashboard of US Accidents: 2016 - 2020
Road traffic crashes are a leading cause of death in the United States for people aged 1–54. The most recent data available from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration shows an average of 6,296,000 police-reported traffic crashes in the United States each year. Nationwide, an average of 3 million people is injured each year in car accidents, and over 90 people die in car accidents every day. In the COVID-19 era, fatal accident percentages are up this year despite the smaller number of vehicles on the roads, NHTSA's latest data shows. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) offers up another reason we need to get rid of COVID-19: “reckless driving is up and causing more deaths.” As the number of car accidents occurring in the United States continues to increase, it can be expected that the number of people who die in car crashes will also continue to increase. This project aims to analyze the US accident data from 50 states to inform the US government agencies and the public on trends and possible causes of traffic accidents and what could be done to reduce them. The analysis includes; Accidents per Day, Percentage High Severity YOY Accidents, YOY Severity, best time to travel by month, day and hour, and factors like weather conditions responsible for accidents nationwide.
Step Name:Create Line and Clustered Chart for Total Accidents by Weather Conditions
Detailed Instructions:

To create Create Line and Clustered Chart for Total Accidents by Weather Conditions please follow the steps below.

Step 1: Weather condition dimension has a lot of values. So the first step here is to group Weather conditions into similar values. On the US_Accidents_Dec20 table, right click on Weather_Condition, then click on New Group. Using the table below as a guide, select the Ungrouped values, click 'Group', then give a name to the Group. (See GIF below)

Note:

Select multiple items by holding the Ctrl button before Grouping
The table below shoes the Group name and the values that should belong to each Group
Click 'OK' when you are done grouping all items

Step 2: In the visualization pane, select the Line and stacked column chart. In the 'MyMeasures' Table, click 'AccidentsPerDay' and 'HighSeverityPerDay' as Column values. Drag and drop %YOYHighSeverity and %YOYAccidentsPerDay into the Line values. Drag and drop %HighSeverity to the Tooltips. In the US_Accidents_Dec20 table, click on 'Weather_Condition (Groups)' and 'Weather_Condition' to add to the Shared axis. (See screenshot below)

Step 3: Format chart using screenshots and GIF below as a guide.

I. General

II. X Axis, Y Axis

III. Data colors

III. Data labels, Shapes, Background and Shadow

IV. Title text

Step 4: Save your report 'Ctrl + S'

Insight: 

For tips on how to create and use combo chart in Power BI, visit: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/visuals/power-bi-visualization-combo-chart

For tips on how to Use grouping and binning in Power BI Desktop, visit: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/create-reports/desktop-grouping-and-binning

Did you complete the Project?

allStepDetails.content:
Power BI Dashboard of US Accidents: 2016 - 2020
Road traffic crashes are a leading cause of death in the United States for people aged 1–54. The most recent data available from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration shows an average of 6,296,000 police-reported traffic crashes in the United States each year. Nationwide, an average of 3 million people is injured each year in car accidents, and over 90 people die in car accidents every day. In the COVID-19 era, fatal accident percentages are up this year despite the smaller number of vehicles on the roads, NHTSA's latest data shows. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) offers up another reason we need to get rid of COVID-19: “reckless driving is up and causing more deaths.” As the number of car accidents occurring in the United States continues to increase, it can be expected that the number of people who die in car crashes will also continue to increase. This project aims to analyze the US accident data from 50 states to inform the US government agencies and the public on trends and possible causes of traffic accidents and what could be done to reduce them. The analysis includes; Accidents per Day, Percentage High Severity YOY Accidents, YOY Severity, best time to travel by month, day and hour, and factors like weather conditions responsible for accidents nationwide.
Step Name:Load CSV Data File to Power BI Desktop
Detailed Instructions:

Step 1: Download File by clicking on the red  icon on top.

Step 2: Extract US_Accidents_Dec20 File to desired location on your computer (See GIF Below)

?

Step 3: Follow GIF Below to Load CSV File to Power BI Desktop

US_Accidents_Dec_20 Table will appear on the Fields pane when load is complete.

Insight: 

Get Power BI Desktop

Connect to CSV files in Power BI Desktop

Power BI Dashboard of US Accidents: 2016 - 2020
Road traffic crashes are a leading cause of death in the United States for people aged 1–54. The most recent data available from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration shows an average of 6,296,000 police-reported traffic crashes in the United States each year. Nationwide, an average of 3 million people is injured each year in car accidents, and over 90 people die in car accidents every day. In the COVID-19 era, fatal accident percentages are up this year despite the smaller number of vehicles on the roads, NHTSA's latest data shows. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) offers up another reason we need to get rid of COVID-19: “reckless driving is up and causing more deaths.” As the number of car accidents occurring in the United States continues to increase, it can be expected that the number of people who die in car crashes will also continue to increase. This project aims to analyze the US accident data from 50 states to inform the US government agencies and the public on trends and possible causes of traffic accidents and what could be done to reduce them. The analysis includes; Accidents per Day, Percentage High Severity YOY Accidents, YOY Severity, best time to travel by month, day and hour, and factors like weather conditions responsible for accidents nationwide.
Step Name:Load Advanced 'Date' Table into model
Detailed Instructions:

The Advanced Date table is a separate table that only contains Dates. It is useful in DAX calculations based on Time Intelligence (eg. SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR). 

The following steps to create an Advanced Date Table to our model can be applied to all your future projects.

Step 1: Download zipped Dates Table M Code by click on the red  icon on top of page

Step 2: Extract Dates Table M code file to your computer and open the file

Step 3: Select All (Ctrl + A) and Copy (Ctrl + C) the codes in the file to your Clipboard

Step 4: Click Transform Data on the Top of Page to open Power Query Editor (See screenshot below)

Step 5: In the Power Query Editor, click New Source, then Blank Query, then click Advanced Editor. Clear any code in the Advanced Editor window, then paste the Dates Table M code previously copied to clipboard to the window. (See GIF below)

Step 6: In the Enter Parameters window, StartDate, EndDate, and FYStartMonth are entered based on the Date range of your Data. In this case our Data ranges from 2016 - 2020, So we type 01/01/2016 as StartDate and 31/12/2020 as EndDate. FYStartMonth (Fiscal Year Start Month) is optional, but in the case we enter 1 (which means January). Note: FYStartMonth may vary based on data and certain requirements. Some business data may start Fiscal Year in March, and in such a case we enter 3 as FYStartMonth . (See screenshot below)

Step 7: Rename Invoked Function as 'Date', press 'Enter' on Keyboard, then 'Close& Apply' to complete. (See GIF below)

Insight: 

Power BI Masterclass - beginners to advanced Microsoft Power BI Created by Dan We on  www.udemy.com was a helpful tool in understanding some concepts of Power BI. Note that there are a number of other free resources out there, but you can check it out below!

https://www.udemy.com/course/power-bi-masterclass-beginners-to-advanced/

Power BI Dashboard of US Accidents: 2016 - 2020
Road traffic crashes are a leading cause of death in the United States for people aged 1–54. The most recent data available from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration shows an average of 6,296,000 police-reported traffic crashes in the United States each year. Nationwide, an average of 3 million people is injured each year in car accidents, and over 90 people die in car accidents every day. In the COVID-19 era, fatal accident percentages are up this year despite the smaller number of vehicles on the roads, NHTSA's latest data shows. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) offers up another reason we need to get rid of COVID-19: “reckless driving is up and causing more deaths.” As the number of car accidents occurring in the United States continues to increase, it can be expected that the number of people who die in car crashes will also continue to increase. This project aims to analyze the US accident data from 50 states to inform the US government agencies and the public on trends and possible causes of traffic accidents and what could be done to reduce them. The analysis includes; Accidents per Day, Percentage High Severity YOY Accidents, YOY Severity, best time to travel by month, day and hour, and factors like weather conditions responsible for accidents nationwide.
Step Name:Load 'Time' table into model
Detailed Instructions:

A Time table is useful in tracking reports at a granular level (eg. the hour or minute of an event). To use a Time table, ensure your data model has a transactional value that includes Time. 

Step 1: Click the Data Icon on the left of your screen, then click New Table on the top (See screenshot below)

Step 2: Copy and Paste the DAX Code below into the window, then click the  icon. (See GIF below)

Time = 
VAR _series =
    GENERATESERIES(1, 1440, 1)
VAR _time =
    ADDCOLUMNS( _series, "TimeandDay", TIME( 0, [Value], 0))
RETURN
    ADDCOLUMNS(
        _time,
        "TimeKey", FORMAT([TimeandDay], "hhmm"),
        "Actual Time", FORMAT([TimeandDay], "HH:MM AM/PM"),
        "Hour", HOUR([TimeandDay]), 
        "Hour Extended", FORMAT([TimeandDay], "H AM/PM"),
        "Minute", MINUTE([TimeandDay]),
        "AMPM", FORMAT([TimeandDay], "AMPM")
    )

Insight: 

Working with time in Power BI Desktop

Power BI Dashboard of US Accidents: 2016 - 2020
Road traffic crashes are a leading cause of death in the United States for people aged 1–54. The most recent data available from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration shows an average of 6,296,000 police-reported traffic crashes in the United States each year. Nationwide, an average of 3 million people is injured each year in car accidents, and over 90 people die in car accidents every day. In the COVID-19 era, fatal accident percentages are up this year despite the smaller number of vehicles on the roads, NHTSA's latest data shows. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) offers up another reason we need to get rid of COVID-19: “reckless driving is up and causing more deaths.” As the number of car accidents occurring in the United States continues to increase, it can be expected that the number of people who die in car crashes will also continue to increase. This project aims to analyze the US accident data from 50 states to inform the US government agencies and the public on trends and possible causes of traffic accidents and what could be done to reduce them. The analysis includes; Accidents per Day, Percentage High Severity YOY Accidents, YOY Severity, best time to travel by month, day and hour, and factors like weather conditions responsible for accidents nationwide.
Step Name:Data Transformation and Relationships
Detailed Instructions:

Step 1: Click on 'Transform Data' to open Power Query Editor (See screenshot)

Step 2: Right click on the Start_Time column then click 'Duplicate Column' (See GIF below)

Step 3: Right click on 'Start_time - Copy', scroll down to 'Transform', then click on 'Time Only'. Scroll across to locate 'Start_Time, right click on 'Start_Time, scroll down to 'Transform', then click on 'Date Only' (See GIF below).

Step 4: Next step is to Group Severity. Severity is grouped from 0 - 5. For this Data, we will group 0,1 as Low Severity, 2 as Average Severity and 3,4 as High Severity.

Right Click on 'Severity', then click on 'Replace values'. Type '0' in 'Value to find' and type '1' in 'Replace With', then click 'OK'.
Right Click on 'Severity' again, then click on ''Replace values', this time type '4' in 'Value to find' and type '3' in 'Replace With' then click 'OK'. (See GIF below).

Step 5: Now it's time to clean our Data and Remove unused columns.

Right click on Start_Time, then click on Rename. Rename Start_Time to Start_Date. Repeat this step for Start_Time - Copy, rename Start_Time - Copy to Start_Time.
To Remove a column, right click on the column then click 'Remove' (See GIF below). Remove all columns except the following:
ID
Start_Date
Start_Time
City 
County
State
Zipcode
Country
Severity
Weather_Condition

Click 'Close & Apply' after removing all unused columns.

Step 6:  Click on the ellipses for the US_Accidents_Dec20 Table then click on New column. Copy and paste DAX equation below, then click  (See screenshot and GIF below)

 TimeKey = FORMAT(US_Accidents_Dec20[Start_Time], "hhmm")

Step 7: Now it's time to create relationships between both tables. Click on the Model icon to show Data Model. Select 'Date' column on the 'Date' Table then drag to the 'Start_Date' column on the 'US_Accident_Dec20' table. Select 'TimeKey' on the 'Time' table the drag to 'TimeKey' on the 'US_Accident_Dec20' table. (See GIF below).

Insight: 

Clean, transform, and load data in Power BI: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/learn/modules/clean-data-power-bi/

Create and manage relationships in Power BI Desktop: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/transform-model/desktop-create-and-manage-relationships

Power BI Dashboard of US Accidents: 2016 - 2020
Road traffic crashes are a leading cause of death in the United States for people aged 1–54. The most recent data available from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration shows an average of 6,296,000 police-reported traffic crashes in the United States each year. Nationwide, an average of 3 million people is injured each year in car accidents, and over 90 people die in car accidents every day. In the COVID-19 era, fatal accident percentages are up this year despite the smaller number of vehicles on the roads, NHTSA's latest data shows. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) offers up another reason we need to get rid of COVID-19: “reckless driving is up and causing more deaths.” As the number of car accidents occurring in the United States continues to increase, it can be expected that the number of people who die in car crashes will also continue to increase. This project aims to analyze the US accident data from 50 states to inform the US government agencies and the public on trends and possible causes of traffic accidents and what could be done to reduce them. The analysis includes; Accidents per Day, Percentage High Severity YOY Accidents, YOY Severity, best time to travel by month, day and hour, and factors like weather conditions responsible for accidents nationwide.
Step Name:Create and format measures for report dashboard
Detailed Instructions:

In Power BI Desktop, Measures calculate a result from an expression formula known as Data Analysis Expressions (DAX) formula language. 

See steps to create and format the measures needed for this report below.

Step 1:  Let us create our first measure; Total Number of Accidents. Right click on 'US_Accidents_Dec20', then click 'New Measure' . Copy and Paste the DAX equation below, then click on the  icon (see GIF below)

TotalAcc = COUNT(US_Accidents_Dec20[ID])

Step 2:  Now let us do a neat trick to keep all our measures organized. Click 'Enter Data' on the home screen, type '1' in the column1, then type 'MyMeasures' in the Name, then click 'Load' (See GIF below)

Now click on our new measure 'TotalAcc', click on the Home table drop down, then move to 'Mymeasures' home table. (see screenshot below)

Now right click on 'Column1' under the 'MyMeasures' table and click delete. leaving only the 'TotalAcc' measure. Then click on show/hide pane to show and hide the Fields tab.  This will refresh the tab and move our 'MyMeasures' table to the top. (See GIF below)

Now we can create all our measures under 'MyMeasures'

Step 3:  See a list of all the measures we need for this dashboard below. For each measure, right click on the 'MyMeasures' table then click 'New Measure', then copy and paste the DAX equation for each measure below followed by the  icon. An example is shown in the GIF below for Accidents Per Day.

List of Measures

1. AccidentsPerDay = [TotalAcc] / COUNT('Date'[Date])

2. LYAccidentPerDay = CALCULATE([AccidentsPerDay], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date]))

3. DiffAccidentPerDay = [AccidentsPerDay] -[LYAccidentPerDay]

4. %YOYAccidentsPerDay = DIVIDE([DiffAccidentPerDay], [LYAccidentPerDay],0)

5. %YOYPerDayIndicator = SWITCH(
    TRUE(), 
    [%YOYAccidentsPerDay]>0, UNICHAR(9650),
    [%YOYAccidentsPerDay]<0, UNICHAR(9660), 
    BLANK())

6. HighSeverity = CALCULATE(COUNT(US_Accidents_Dec20[Severity]), FILTER(US_Accidents_Dec20, US_Accidents_Dec20[Severity] = 3))

7. %HighSeverity = DIVIDE([HighSeverity],[TotalAcc])

8. LYHighSeverity = CALCULATE([HighSeverity], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date]))

9. DiffHighSeverity = [HighSeverity] - [LYHighSeverity]

10. %YOYHighSeverity = DIVIDE([DiffHighSeverity], [LYHighSeverity], 0)

11. HighSeverityPerDay = [%HighSeverity] * [AccidentsPerDay]

12. %YOYHSIndicator = SWITCH(
    TRUE(), 
    [%YOYHighSeverity]>0, UNICHAR(9650),
    [%YOYHighSeverity]<0, UNICHAR(9660), 
    BLANK())

Step 4:  After creating the measures, some of them needs to be formatted. To format a measure, click on the measure, then the 'Measure tools' will appear on top of the page, then you can format based on your preference and click on the  icon when done. An example is shown in the GIF below for %HighSeverity. Here we will format by clicking on the % sign and reducing the decimal places to 1.  Repeat the steps in the GIF below to format '%YOYAccidentsPerDay' and '%YOYHighSeverity' 

See screenshot below to format AccidentsPerDay. Repeat the steps to format LYAccidentPerDay, DiffAccidentPerDay, DiffHighSeverity, HighSeverity, LYHighSeverity, and HighSeverityPerDay.  

 
Insight: 

Create Measures for Data analysis in Power BI Desktop

Power BI Masterclass - beginners to advanced Microsoft Power BI Created by Dan We on  www.udemy.com was a helpful tool in understanding some concepts of Power BI. Note that there are a number of other free resources out there, but you can check it out below!

https://www.udemy.com/course/power-bi-masterclass-beginners-to-advanced/

Power BI Dashboard of US Accidents: 2016 - 2020
Road traffic crashes are a leading cause of death in the United States for people aged 1–54. The most recent data available from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration shows an average of 6,296,000 police-reported traffic crashes in the United States each year. Nationwide, an average of 3 million people is injured each year in car accidents, and over 90 people die in car accidents every day. In the COVID-19 era, fatal accident percentages are up this year despite the smaller number of vehicles on the roads, NHTSA's latest data shows. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) offers up another reason we need to get rid of COVID-19: “reckless driving is up and causing more deaths.” As the number of car accidents occurring in the United States continues to increase, it can be expected that the number of people who die in car crashes will also continue to increase. This project aims to analyze the US accident data from 50 states to inform the US government agencies and the public on trends and possible causes of traffic accidents and what could be done to reduce them. The analysis includes; Accidents per Day, Percentage High Severity YOY Accidents, YOY Severity, best time to travel by month, day and hour, and factors like weather conditions responsible for accidents nationwide.
Step Name:Create and format measures for report dashboard
Detailed Instructions:

In Power BI Desktop, Measures calculate a result from an expression formula known as Data Analysis Expressions (DAX) formula language. 

See steps to create and format the measures needed for this report below.

Step 1:  Let us create our first measure; Total Number of Accidents. Right click on 'US_Accidents_Dec20', then click 'New Measure' . Copy and Paste the DAX equation below, then click on the  icon (see GIF below)

TotalAcc = COUNT(US_Accidents_Dec20[ID])

Step 2:  Now let us do a neat trick to keep all our measures organized. Click 'Enter Data' on the home screen, type '1' in the column1, then type 'MyMeasures' in the Name, then click 'Load' (See GIF below)

Now click on our new measure 'TotalAcc', click on the Home table drop down, then move to 'Mymeasures' home table. (see screenshot below)

Now right click on 'Column1' under the 'MyMeasures' table and click delete. leaving only the 'TotalAcc' measure. Then click on show/hide pane to show and hide the Fields tab.  This will refresh the tab and move our 'MyMeasures' table to the top. (See GIF below)

Now we can create all our measures under 'MyMeasures'

Step 3:  See a list of all the measures we need for this dashboard below. For each measure, right click on the 'MyMeasures' table then click 'New Measure', then copy and paste the DAX equation for each measure below followed by the  icon. An example is shown in the GIF below for Accidents Per Day.

List of Measures

1. AccidentsPerDay = [TotalAcc] / COUNT('Date'[Date])

2. LYAccidentPerDay = CALCULATE([AccidentsPerDay], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date]))

3. DiffAccidentPerDay = [AccidentsPerDay] -[LYAccidentPerDay]

4. %YOYAccidentsPerDay = DIVIDE([DiffAccidentPerDay], [LYAccidentPerDay],0)

5. %YOYPerDayIndicator = SWITCH(
    TRUE(), 
    [%YOYAccidentsPerDay]>0, UNICHAR(9650),
    [%YOYAccidentsPerDay]<0, UNICHAR(9660), 
    BLANK())

6. HighSeverity = CALCULATE(COUNT(US_Accidents_Dec20[Severity]), FILTER(US_Accidents_Dec20, US_Accidents_Dec20[Severity] = 3))

7. %HighSeverity = DIVIDE([HighSeverity],[TotalAcc])

8. LYHighSeverity = CALCULATE([HighSeverity], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date]))

9. DiffHighSeverity = [HighSeverity] - [LYHighSeverity]

10. %YOYHighSeverity = DIVIDE([DiffHighSeverity], [LYHighSeverity], 0)

11. HighSeverityPerDay = [%HighSeverity] * [AccidentsPerDay]

12. %YOYHSIndicator = SWITCH(
    TRUE(), 
    [%YOYHighSeverity]>0, UNICHAR(9650),
    [%YOYHighSeverity]<0, UNICHAR(9660), 
    BLANK())

Step 4:  After creating the measures, some of them needs to be formatted. To format a measure, click on the measure, then the 'Measure tools' will appear on top of the page, then you can format based on your preference and click on the  icon when done. An example is shown in the GIF below for %HighSeverity. Here we will format by clicking on the % sign and reducing the decimal places to 1.  Repeat the steps in the GIF below to format '%YOYAccidentsPerDay' and '%YOYHighSeverity' 

See screenshot below to format AccidentsPerDay. Repeat the steps to format LYAccidentPerDay, DiffAccidentPerDay, DiffHighSeverity, HighSeverity, LYHighSeverity, and HighSeverityPerDay.  

 
Insight: 

Create Measures for Data analysis in Power BI Desktop

To format a measure, click on the measure, then the 'Measure tools' will appear on top of the page, then you can format based on your preference and click on the  icon when done. An example is shown in the GIF below for %HighSeverity. Here we will format by clicking on the % sign and reducing the decimal places to 1.  Repeat the steps in the GIF below to format '%YOYAccidentsPerDay' and '%YOYHighSeverity' 

See screenshot below to format AccidentsPerDay. Repeat the steps to format LYAccidentPerDay, DiffAccidentPerDay, DiffHighSeverity, HighSeverity, LYHighSeverity, and HighSeverityPerDay.  

 
Insight: 

Create Measures for Data analysis in Power BI Desktop

Power BI Masterclass - beginners to advanced Microsoft Power BI Created by Dan We on  www.udemy.com was a helpful tool in understanding some concepts of Power BI. Note that there are a number of other free resources out there, but you can check it out below!

https://www.udemy.com/course/power-bi-masterclass-beginners-to-advanced/

Power BI Dashboard of US Accidents: 2016 - 2020
Road traffic crashes are a leading cause of death in the United States for people aged 1–54. The most recent data available from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration shows an average of 6,296,000 police-reported traffic crashes in the United States each year. Nationwide, an average of 3 million people is injured each year in car accidents, and over 90 people die in car accidents every day. In the COVID-19 era, fatal accident percentages are up this year despite the smaller number of vehicles on the roads, NHTSA's latest data shows. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) offers up another reason we need to get rid of COVID-19: “reckless driving is up and causing more deaths.” As the number of car accidents occurring in the United States continues to increase, it can be expected that the number of people who die in car crashes will also continue to increase. This project aims to analyze the US accident data from 50 states to inform the US government agencies and the public on trends and possible causes of traffic accidents and what could be done to reduce them. The analysis includes; Accidents per Day, Percentage High Severity YOY Accidents, YOY Severity, best time to travel by month, day and hour, and factors like weather conditions responsible for accidents nationwide.
Step Name:Create and format measures for report dashboard
Detailed Instructions:

In Power BI Desktop, Measures calculate a result from an expression formula known as Data Analysis Expressions (DAX) formula language. 

See steps to create and format the measures needed for this report below.

Step 1:  Let us create our first measure; Total Number of Accidents. Right click on 'US_Accidents_Dec20', then click 'New Measure' . Copy and Paste the DAX equation below, then click on the  icon (see GIF below)

TotalAcc = COUNT(US_Accidents_Dec20[ID])

Step 2:  Now let us do a neat trick to keep all our measures organized. Click 'Enter Data' on the home screen, type '1' in the column1, then type 'MyMeasures' in the Name, then click 'Load' (See GIF below)

Now click on our new measure 'TotalAcc', click on the Home table drop down, then move to 'Mymeasures' home table. (see screenshot below)

Now right click on 'Column1' under the 'MyMeasures' table and click delete. leaving only the 'TotalAcc' measure. Then click on show/hide pane to show and hide the Fields tab.  This will refresh the tab and move our 'MyMeasures' table to the top. (See GIF below)

Now we can create all our measures under 'MyMeasures'

Step 3:  See a list of all the measures we need for this dashboard below. For each measure, right click on the 'MyMeasures' table then click 'New Measure', then copy and paste the DAX equation for each measure below followed by the  icon. An example is shown in the GIF below for Accidents Per Day.

List of Measures

1. AccidentsPerDay = [TotalAcc] / COUNT('Date'[Date])

2. LYAccidentPerDay = CALCULATE([AccidentsPerDay], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date]))

3. DiffAccidentPerDay = [AccidentsPerDay] -[LYAccidentPerDay]

4. %YOYAccidentsPerDay = DIVIDE([DiffAccidentPerDay], [LYAccidentPerDay],0)

5. %YOYPerDayIndicator = SWITCH(
    TRUE(), 
    [%YOYAccidentsPerDay]>0, UNICHAR(9650),
    [%YOYAccidentsPerDay]<0, UNICHAR(9660), 
    BLANK())

6. HighSeverity = CALCULATE(COUNT(US_Accidents_Dec20[Severity]), FILTER(US_Accidents_Dec20, US_Accidents_Dec20[Severity] = 3))

7. %HighSeverity = DIVIDE([HighSeverity],[TotalAcc])

8. LYHighSeverity = CALCULATE([HighSeverity], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date]))

9. DiffHighSeverity = [HighSeverity] - [LYHighSeverity]

10. %YOYHighSeverity = DIVIDE([DiffHighSeverity], [LYHighSeverity], 0)

11. HighSeverityPerDay = [%HighSeverity] * [AccidentsPerDay]

12. %YOYHSIndicator = SWITCH(
    TRUE(), 
    [%YOYHighSeverity]>0, UNICHAR(9650),
    [%YOYHighSeverity]<0, UNICHAR(9660), 
    BLANK())

Step 4:  After creating the measures, some of them needs to be formatted. To format a measure, click on the measure, then the 'Measure tools' will appear on top of the page, then you can format based on your preference and click on the  icon when done. An example is shown in the GIF below for %HighSeverity. Here we will format by clicking on the % sign and reducing the decimal places to 1.  Repeat the steps in the GIF below to format '%YOYAccidentsPerDay' and '%YOYHighSeverity' 

See screenshot below to format AccidentsPerDay. Repeat the steps to format LYAccidentPerDay, DiffAccidentPerDay, DiffHighSeverity, HighSeverity, LYHighSeverity, and HighSeverityPerDay.  

 
Insight: 

Create Measures for Data analysis in Power BI Desktop

    [%YOYHighSeverity]<0, UNICHAR(9660), 
    BLANK())

Step 4:  After creating the measures, some of them needs to be formatted. To format a measure, click on the measure, then the 'Measure tools' will appear on top of the page, then you can format based on your preference and click on the  icon when done. An example is shown in the GIF below for %HighSeverity. Here we will format by clicking on the % sign and reducing the decimal places to 1.  Repeat the steps in the GIF below to format '%YOYAccidentsPerDay' and '%YOYHighSeverity' 

See screenshot below to format AccidentsPerDay. Repeat the steps to format LYAccidentPerDay, DiffAccidentPerDay, DiffHighSeverity, HighSeverity, LYHighSeverity, and HighSeverityPerDay.

Step Name:Create dashboard background, theme, and add logo to report
Detailed Instructions:

We will like to create background image, set the theme, and add the Colaberry logo in our report. Note that you can use any image or theme based on your preference. 

See steps below.

Step 1: Download background image and logo by click on the red  icon. 

Step 2: Click on the 'format'; icon on the home page, then click on 'Page background', then click on 'Add image', locate the image file on your computer and click open (see GIF below)

Step 3: Set 'Transparency' to 30% and 'Image fit' to Fit (see screenshot below)

Step 4: Now let us set Report theme. Click on 'View' on top of the page, then click on the dropdown list, then select the preferred Theme (see screenshot below)

Step 5: Copy and paste the logo to your dashboard page and follow the format settings shown in the screenshots below:

Now let us create our third chart with is a Line and clustered chart for Total Accidents by Time. Please follow detailed steps below to achieve the same results.

Power BI Dashboard of US Accidents: 2016 - 2020
Road traffic crashes are a leading cause of death in the United States for people aged 1–54. The most recent data available from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration shows an average of 6,296,000 police-reported traffic crashes in the United States each year. Nationwide, an average of 3 million people is injured each year in car accidents, and over 90 people die in car accidents every day. In the COVID-19 era, fatal accident percentages are up this year despite the smaller number of vehicles on the roads, NHTSA's latest data shows. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) offers up another reason we need to get rid of COVID-19: “reckless driving is up and causing more deaths.” As the number of car accidents occurring in the United States continues to increase, it can be expected that the number of people who die in car crashes will also continue to increase. This project aims to analyze the US accident data from 50 states to inform the US government agencies and the public on trends and possible causes of traffic accidents and what could be done to reduce them. The analysis includes; Accidents per Day, Percentage High Severity YOY Accidents, YOY Severity, best time to travel by month, day and hour, and factors like weather conditions responsible for accidents nationwide.
Step Name:Create Line and Clustered Chart for Total Accidents by Weather Conditions
Detailed Instructions:

To create Create Line and Clustered Chart for Total Accidents by Weather Conditions please follow the steps below.

Step 1: Weather condition dimension has a lot of values. So the first step here is to group Weather conditions into similar values. On the US_Accidents_Dec20 table, right click on Weather_Condition, then click on New Group. Using the table below as a guide, select the Ungrouped values, click 'Group', then give a name to the Group. (See GIF below)

Note:

Select multiple items by holding the Ctrl button before Grouping
The table below shoes the Group name and the values that should belong to each Group
Click 'OK' when you are done grouping all items

Step 2: In the visualization pane, select the Line and stacked column chart. In the 'MyMeasures' Table, click 'AccidentsPerDay' and 'HighSeverityPerDay' as Column values. Drag and drop %YOYHighSeverity and %YOYAccidentsPerDay into the Line values. Drag and drop %HighSeverity to the Tooltips. In the US_Accidents_Dec20 table, click on 'Weather_Condition (Groups)' and 'Weather_Condition' to add to the Shared axis. (See screenshot below)

Step 3: Format chart using screenshots and GIF below as a guide.

I. General

II. X Axis, Y Axis

III. Data colors

III. Data labels, Shapes, Background and Shadow

IV. Title text

Step 4: Save your report 'Ctrl + S'

Insight: 

For tips on how to create and use combo chart in Power BI, visit: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/visuals/power-bi-visualization-combo-chart

For tips on how to Use grouping and binning in Power BI Desktop, visit: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/create-reports/desktop-grouping-and-binning

portfolioContent.title: Power BI Dashboard of US Accidents: 2016 - 2020
portfolioContent.portfolioSummary: Developed a Power BI dashboard analyzing US traffic accident trends to support data-driven safety interventions.
portfolioContent.summary: This project analyzed US traffic accident data from 2016 to 2020 using Power BI to identify trends, severity, and contributing factors such as weather conditions. The dashboard provides insights on accident frequency by time and severity to inform government agencies and the public. It highlights critical patterns to aid in reducing traffic-related injuries and fatalities.
portfolioContent.businessProblem: Road traffic crashes are a leading cause of death in the US for ages 1–54, with millions injured and over 90 daily fatalities. Despite fewer vehicles during COVID-19, fatal accidents increased due to reckless driving. There is a need for detailed analysis to understand accident trends and causes to improve road safety.
objectives: Analyze accident frequency and severity trends across all 50 states
Identify temporal patterns for safer travel times by month, day, and hour
Assess impact of weather conditions on accident occurrence and severity
tools: National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) data
workflow: Gather and preprocess US accident data from NHTSA
Group weather conditions into meaningful categories
Create visualizations including line and clustered column charts
Analyze accidents per day, severity, and year-over-year changes
Develop and format an interactive Power BI dashboard for insights
keyInsights: Fatal accident percentages increased during COVID-19 despite fewer vehicles on roads
Reckless driving contributed significantly to the rise in fatal accidents
Certain weather conditions correlate with higher accident frequencies
Best travel times vary by month, day, and hour to minimize accident risk
businessImpact: Informed government agencies and public on critical accident trends
Provided actionable insights to support targeted road safety measures
Enabled data-driven decisions to reduce traffic injuries and fatalities
